name: Vortex Points
description: Vortex points, also known as energy vortexes or power places, are locations on Earth believed to possess concentrated energy fields or spiritual power. Proponents claim these points can act as portals to other dimensions, facilitate healing, enhance psychic abilities, and offer profound spiritual experiences. The concept draws from various traditions including geomancy, dowsing, and New Age spiritualism. Key evidence cited includes anecdotal accounts of heightened experiences at specific locations like Sedona, Arizona; Stonehenge, England; and Machu Picchu, Peru. Some proponents interpret geological features, ley lines, and ancient monuments as evidence of deliberate construction aligning with these energy flows. Mainstream science generally dismisses vortex points as lacking empirical support. Geologists explain unusual phenomena at these locations through natural processes like magnetic anomalies, geological formations, or atmospheric conditions. Psychologists attribute reported experiences to placebo effects, suggestibility, and psychological factors related to the environment. Archaeologists and historians often offer alternative interpretations of ancient sites, focusing on practical and cultural purposes rather than alleged energetic properties. While some researchers explore the influence of environmental factors on human consciousness, the specific claims of vortex points as portals or concentrated energy fields remain outside the scope of accepted scientific inquiry. The enduring popularity of the vortex point concept reflects a continued interest in alternative spiritualities and the search for meaning in the natural world.
subtitle: Locations of concentrated Earth energy.

claims.claim: Vortex points are locations of heightened energy.
claims.evidence: Personal experiences, dowsing results, alleged Kirlian photography evidence.
claims.criticism: Lack of quantifiable evidence, placebo effect, dowsing debunked as pseudoscience, Kirlian photography measures electrical discharge, not 'energy'.

claims.claim: Vortex points can act as portals to other dimensions.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts of altered states of consciousness, alleged sightings of interdimensional beings.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable evidence, subjective experiences open to interpretation, no scientific basis for interdimensional travel.

claims.claim: Vortex points enhance psychic abilities and healing.
claims.evidence: Claims of improved intuition, spontaneous healings reported at specific locations.
claims.criticism: Anecdotal evidence, lack of controlled studies, alternative explanations for healing (e.g., placebo effect, stress reduction).

keyFigures.name: Sig Lonegren
keyFigures.role: Promoter of geomancy and ley line research.

keyFigures.name: Paul Devereux
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist critical of unscientific claims regarding ancient sites.

keyFigures.name: Martin Gray
keyFigures.role: Photographer and author documenting sacred sites globally.

timeline.event: Indigenous cultures identify and venerate specific locations as sacred.
Alfred Watkins popularizes the concept of ley lines in England.
New Age movement embraces vortex points as locations for spiritual growth and healing.

sources.author: Paul Devereux
sources.work: Earth Memory: Practical Examples Using Sacred Geography
sources.title: Paul Devereux
